steps.title: What Are Conic Sections?
steps.goal: See how a plane slicing through a double cone creates different curves.
steps.info: The same four shapes — circles, ellipses, parabolas, and hyperbolas — show up in orbits, architecture, and optics. They all start here: a plane meeting a cone.
steps.introText: Conic sections are shapes created when a flat plane cuts through a double cone. By changing the angle of the plane, we can create circles, ellipses, parabolas, and hyperbolas.
steps.prompt: Toggle the labels to identify the cone, plane, and cross section, then continue when you're ready.

steps.title: Free Exploration
steps.goal: Discover that different slices produce different shapes.
steps.info: Try combining tilt and height. A nearly flat plane gives a circle; tilting more stretches it into an ellipse, then a parabola, and eventually a hyperbola when the plane cuts both cones.
steps.prompt: Move the plane around and watch the cross section update in real time.
hints: What happens if the plane cuts both cones?

steps.title: Create a Circle
steps.goal: Make the cross-section a circle, then press Check.
steps.info: A circle forms when the plane is almost horizontal and cuts across one cone — like slicing an ice-cream cone straight across.

feedback.correct: Great! A circle is formed when the plane cuts straight across one cone.
feedback.incorrect: This slice isn't a circle yet.
feedback.hint: Try making the plane more horizontal (near 0° or 180°) and keep the plane in the upper or lower cone.

steps.title: Create an Ellipse
steps.goal: Tilt the plane slightly so the slice becomes an oval.
steps.info: An ellipse appears when the plane is tilted but still shallow enough to cut only one cone. Think of it as a stretched circle.

feedback.correct: Nice work! An ellipse forms when the plane is tilted but still cuts only one cone.
feedback.incorrect: You're close, but this slice is producing a different conic.
feedback.hint: Tilt the plane slightly without making it parallel to the side of the cone.

steps.miniReflection: Notice how the circle stretched into an oval shape.

steps.title: Create a Parabola
steps.goal: Match the plane's angle to the slope of the cone's edge.
steps.info: A parabola is the borderline case: the plane is parallel to the cone's side, so it never cuts through to the other side of the cone.

feedback.correct: Excellent! A parabola appears when the plane is parallel to the cone's side.
feedback.hint: Match the slope of the cone's edge. In the upper cone, try ~53° or ~127°.

steps.title: Create a Hyperbola
steps.goal: Steepen the plane so it cuts through both cones.
steps.info: A hyperbola has two separate branches because the plane slices through both halves of the double cone. Move near the apex, then tilt steeply.

feedback.correct: Perfect! Because the plane cuts both cones, the cross section becomes a hyperbola.
feedback.hint: Can you make the plane pass through both cones?

steps.title: Check Your Understanding
steps.goal: Connect what you did with the big idea behind conic sections.
steps.info: The plane's angle relative to the cone is what matters — not the cone's size, color, or how we draw it on screen.
steps.prompt: What determines which conic section appears?
steps.feedback: Exactly. The angle of the plane relative to the cone determines the shape.

steps.title: Mastery Check
steps.goal: Prove you can create all four conic sections without hints.
steps.info: Scientists and engineers use these same shapes to model planetary orbits (ellipses), satellite dishes (parabolas), and cooling towers (hyperbolas).
steps.completionMessage: You've discovered all four conic sections! In the next lesson, we'll focus on parabolas and see how they appear everywhere from satellite dishes to car headlights.
